---
title: "Chapter 2: R Building Blocks"
subtitle: "R for Management Consultants | BK Advisors"
author: "BK Advisors"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    logo: bka-logo.png
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: slide
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#005CB9"
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

::: {.incremental}
- Create **vectors** to store collections of related MNH data values
- Distinguish the four core **data types** in R: numeric, character, logical, and factor
- Use **comparison and logical operators** to test conditions on health data
- Write **custom functions** to automate routine indicator calculations
- Use R's **help system** and online resources to solve problems independently
:::


## Chapter Overview

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #E24A3F;">Vectors</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;">Data<br>Types</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;">Operators</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #FA7650;">Functions</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;">Getting<br>Help</div>
</div>


# Vectors {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Storing collections of related values</span>


## What Is a Vector?

::: {.definition-box}
A **vector** is R's most fundamental data structure --- an ordered collection of values of the same type. Nearly everything in R is built on vectors.
:::

. . .

```{r}
#| label: first-vector
#| echo: true

# A single value is actually a vector of length 1
neonatal_deaths <- 23
length(neonatal_deaths)  # 1
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: multi-vector
#| echo: true

# A vector of neonatal mortality rates (per 1,000 live births)
nmr <- c(25.3, 31.7, 19.8, 42.1, 27.5)
nmr
length(nmr)  # 5
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**`c()` stands for "combine."** It's the function you'll use most often to create vectors by hand.
:::


## Named Vectors

Names make vectors self-documenting --- you always know which value belongs to which entity.

```{r}
#| label: named-vectors
#| echo: true

# NMR by country (per 1,000 live births)
nmr <- c(
  Ethiopia    = 25.3,
  Tanzania    = 31.7,
  Malawi      = 19.8,
  Mozambique  = 42.1,
  Kenya       = 27.5
)
nmr
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: named-vectors-2
#| echo: true

# Monthly deliveries at a facility
deliveries <- c(
  Jan = 112, Feb = 98,  Mar = 134,
  Apr = 121, May = 105, Jun = 143
)
deliveries
```


## Vector Operations

R operates on entire vectors at once --- no loops needed.

```{r}
#| label: vector-ops
#| echo: true

nmr <- c(Ethiopia = 25.3, Tanzania = 31.7, Malawi = 19.8,
         Mozambique = 42.1, Kenya = 27.5)

# Summary statistics
mean(nmr)     # average NMR across countries
sum(nmr)      # total (less meaningful here, but useful for counts)
min(nmr)      # best performing country
max(nmr)      # worst performing country
range(nmr)    # both min and max
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: vectorized-math
#| echo: true

# Vectorized math: operation applies to every element
live_births <- c(1200, 950, 1100, 800, 1050)
neonatal_deaths <- c(30, 30, 22, 34, 29)

# Calculate NMR for each country
nmr_calculated <- (neonatal_deaths / live_births) * 1000
nmr_calculated
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Vectorized operations are R's superpower.** One line of code replaces five rows of Excel formulas --- and there's no risk of a formula not being copied down correctly.
:::


## Indexing by Position

Use square brackets `[ ]` to extract specific elements from a vector.

```{r}
#| label: index-position
#| echo: true

nmr <- c(Ethiopia = 25.3, Tanzania = 31.7, Malawi = 19.8,
         Mozambique = 42.1, Kenya = 27.5)

# Single element (first country)
nmr[1]

# Multiple elements (first and third)
nmr[c(1, 3)]

# A range (second through fourth)
nmr[2:4]

# Exclude an element (drop Mozambique)
nmr[-4]
```


## Indexing by Name

Named vectors can be indexed by name --- often clearer than positions.

```{r}
#| label: index-name
#| echo: true

nmr <- c(Ethiopia = 25.3, Tanzania = 31.7, Malawi = 19.8,
         Mozambique = 42.1, Kenya = 27.5)

# Single country
nmr["Ethiopia"]

# Multiple countries
nmr[c("Malawi", "Kenya")]
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Prefer named indexing over position indexing.** `nmr["Ethiopia"]` is self-explanatory; `nmr[1]` requires you to remember that Ethiopia is the first element.
:::


## Indexing by Condition

The most powerful form of indexing: use a logical test to select elements.

```{r}
#| label: index-condition
#| echo: true

nmr <- c(Ethiopia = 25.3, Tanzania = 31.7, Malawi = 19.8,
         Mozambique = 42.1, Kenya = 27.5)

# Countries with NMR above 30
nmr[nmr > 30]

# Countries at or below the SDG target (12 per 1,000)
nmr[nmr <= 12]

# Countries NOT meeting the target
nmr[nmr > 12]
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: index-condition-2
#| echo: true

# Which countries have high NMR?
names(nmr[nmr > 30])

# How many countries exceed 25?
sum(nmr > 25)
```


## Modifying Vectors

```{r}
#| label: modify-vectors
#| echo: true

nmr <- c(Ethiopia = 25.3, Tanzania = 31.7, Malawi = 19.8,
         Mozambique = 42.1, Kenya = 27.5)

# Update a value (new data comes in for Tanzania)
nmr["Tanzania"] <- 29.4
nmr

# Add a new country
nmr["Uganda"] <- 22.1
nmr

# Sort from lowest to highest
sort(nmr)

# Sort from highest to lowest
sort(nmr, decreasing = TRUE)
```


# Data Types {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Numeric, character, logical, and factor</span>


## The Four Core Data Types

Every value in R has a type. Using the right type ensures R handles your data correctly.

| Type | What It Stores | MNH Example |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| **numeric** | Numbers (decimal or integer) | NMR = 25.3, deliveries = 1200 |
| **character** | Text strings | "Ethiopia", "Hospital" |
| **logical** | TRUE or FALSE | has_cpap = TRUE |
| **factor** | Categories with a fixed set of levels | facility_type: Health Post, Health Center, Hospital |

. . .

```{r}
#| label: check-types
#| echo: true

# Check the type of any value
class(25.3)           # "numeric"
class("Ethiopia")     # "character"
class(TRUE)           # "logical"
```


## Numeric: The Workhorse

Most MNH data is numeric --- rates, counts, percentages.

```{r}
#| label: numeric-type
#| echo: true

# Mortality rates (per 1,000 live births)
nmr <- c(25.3, 31.7, 19.8, 42.1, 27.5)

# Delivery counts
deliveries <- c(1200L, 950L, 1100L, 800L, 1050L)  # L = integer

# Check types
class(nmr)          # "numeric"
class(deliveries)   # "integer"
is.numeric(nmr)     # TRUE
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Numeric covers both decimals and integers.** Adding `L` after a number forces it to be an integer, but in practice this distinction rarely matters for analysis.
:::


## Character: Text Data

Character vectors store any text: country names, facility names, notes.

```{r}
#| label: character-type
#| echo: true

# Country names
countries <- c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Mozambique", "Kenya")

# Facility identifiers
facility_ids <- c("ETH-HC-042", "TZA-HOSP-007", "MWI-HP-118")

# Common operations on character vectors
nchar(countries)                  # number of characters in each
toupper(countries)                # convert to uppercase
paste(countries, "NMR:", nmr)     # combine text with numbers
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Numbers stored as text cause silent errors.** If you read "25.3" from a CSV, R may treat it as character. Use `as.numeric()` to convert: `as.numeric("25.3")` gives `25.3`.
:::


## Logical: TRUE / FALSE

Logical vectors arise from comparisons and represent yes/no conditions.

```{r}
#| label: logical-type
#| echo: true

# Equipment availability
has_cpap <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
has_ultrasound <- c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)

# Logical vectors from comparisons
nmr <- c(Ethiopia = 25.3, Tanzania = 31.7, Malawi = 19.8,
         Mozambique = 42.1, Kenya = 27.5)

high_nmr <- nmr > 30
high_nmr   # FALSE TRUE FALSE TRUE FALSE
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: logical-tricks
#| echo: true

# Useful trick: TRUE = 1, FALSE = 0
sum(has_cpap)                  # count of TRUE values: 3
mean(has_cpap)                 # proportion with CPAP: 0.6
mean(has_cpap) * 100           # percentage with CPAP: 60
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**`sum(logical_vector)` counts how many are TRUE. `mean(logical_vector)` gives the proportion.** This pattern is used constantly in MNH indicator calculations.
:::


## Factor: Categorical Data

Factors store categorical variables with a fixed set of possible values (levels).

```{r}
#| label: factor-type
#| echo: true

# Facility types as a simple character vector
facility_type <- c("Hospital", "Health Center", "Health Post",
                   "Health Center", "Hospital")

# Convert to factor
facility_type <- factor(facility_type)
facility_type
levels(facility_type)  # alphabetical by default
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: factor-ordered
#| echo: true

# Ordered factor: levels in a meaningful sequence
facility_type <- factor(
  c("Hospital", "Health Center", "Health Post",
    "Health Center", "Hospital"),
  levels = c("Health Post", "Health Center", "Hospital"),
  ordered = TRUE
)
facility_type
# Health Post < Health Center < Hospital
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Why not just use character?** Factors control the order of categories in charts and tables. Without a factor, ggplot2 sorts alphabetically --- "Health Post" would appear before "Hospital" instead of in the logical service-level order.
:::


## Handling Missing Values: NA

Real-world MNH data always has gaps. R uses `NA` ("Not Available") to represent missing values.

```{r}
#| label: na-handling
#| echo: true

# Some facilities didn't report deliveries this month
deliveries <- c(112, 98, NA, 121, NA, 143)

# NA is contagious — any operation involving NA returns NA
mean(deliveries)        # NA!
sum(deliveries)         # NA!
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: na-handling-2
#| echo: true

# Use na.rm = TRUE to remove NAs before calculating
mean(deliveries, na.rm = TRUE)    # 118.5
sum(deliveries, na.rm = TRUE)     # 474

# Check for missing values
is.na(deliveries)                 # FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE TRUE FALSE
sum(is.na(deliveries))            # 2 missing values
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .red}
**Never replace NA with 0 without justification.** A facility that reported zero deliveries is very different from a facility that didn't report at all. Treating missing data as zero inflates denominators and deflates rates.
:::


# Comparison & Logical Operators {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Testing conditions on your data</span>


## Comparison Operators

Comparison operators test a condition and return `TRUE` or `FALSE`.

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `==` | Equal to | `nmr == 25.3` |
| `!=` | Not equal to | `country != "Ethiopia"` |
| `>` | Greater than | `nmr > 30` |
| `<` | Less than | `nmr < 12` |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal | `deliveries >= 100` |
| `<=` | Less than or equal | `nmr <= 25` |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Common mistake: using `=` instead of `==`.** A single `=` is assignment (like `<-`). Double `==` is comparison. `nmr = 30` sets nmr to 30; `nmr == 30` tests whether nmr equals 30.
:::


## Comparison Operators in Action

```{r}
#| label: comparison-ops
#| echo: true

nmr <- c(Ethiopia = 25.3, Tanzania = 31.7, Malawi = 19.8,
         Mozambique = 42.1, Kenya = 27.5)

# Which countries have NMR above the SDG target of 12?
nmr > 12        # all TRUE in this set

# Which countries have NMR above 30?
nmr > 30        # FALSE TRUE FALSE TRUE FALSE

# Which country has the lowest NMR?
nmr == min(nmr)  # FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE FALSE --- Malawi
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: comparison-ops-2
#| echo: true

# Practical: identify priority countries
countries <- c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Mozambique", "Kenya")

# Countries where NMR exceeds 30
countries[nmr > 30]     # "Tanzania" "Mozambique"

# Countries at or below 25
countries[nmr <= 25]    # "Malawi"
```


## Logical Operators

Combine multiple conditions using logical operators.

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `&` | AND (both must be TRUE) | `nmr > 25 & deliveries > 100` |
| `|` | OR (at least one TRUE) | `nmr > 40 | deliveries < 50` |
| `!` | NOT (flip TRUE/FALSE) | `!has_cpap` |

. . .

```{r}
#| label: logical-ops
#| echo: true

nmr <- c(Ethiopia = 25.3, Tanzania = 31.7, Malawi = 19.8,
         Mozambique = 42.1, Kenya = 27.5)
has_cpap <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)

# High NMR AND no CPAP — highest priority
high_risk <- (nmr > 30) & (!has_cpap)
high_risk   # FALSE TRUE FALSE TRUE FALSE

# Which countries?
names(nmr)[high_risk]   # "Tanzania" "Mozambique"
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Combining conditions is how you identify priority facilities in practice.** High mortality, low equipment, low staffing --- the intersection of multiple risk factors drives resource allocation decisions.
:::


## The %in% Operator

Test whether values appear in a set --- cleaner than chaining `|` conditions.

```{r}
#| label: in-operator
#| echo: true

countries <- c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Mozambique", "Kenya")

# East African countries
east_africa <- c("Ethiopia", "Kenya", "Tanzania")

# Which are in East Africa?
countries %in% east_africa
# TRUE TRUE FALSE FALSE TRUE

# Filter to East African countries
countries[countries %in% east_africa]
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**`%in%` is essential for filtering.** Instead of writing `country == "Ethiopia" | country == "Kenya" | country == "Tanzania"`, write `country %in% c("Ethiopia", "Kenya", "Tanzania")`.
:::


# Functions {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Reusable calculations for routine tasks</span>


## Using Built-in Functions

You've already been using functions: `c()`, `mean()`, `sum()`, `length()`.

```{r}
#| label: builtin-functions
#| echo: true

nmr <- c(25.3, 31.7, 19.8, 42.1, 27.5)

# Functions follow the pattern: function_name(arguments)
mean(nmr)                  # one argument
round(mean(nmr), 1)        # nested: round the mean to 1 decimal
seq(0, 50, by = 10)        # named argument: sequence from 0 to 50
paste("NMR:", nmr)          # combine text with values
```

. . .

::: {.definition-box}
A **function** takes inputs (arguments), does something with them, and returns an output. Think of it like a formula in Excel, but more flexible and reusable.
:::


## Anatomy of a Function Call

```{r}
#| label: function-anatomy
#| echo: true

# round(x, digits)
#   |     |    |
#   |     |    └── argument 2: how many decimals
#   |     └─────── argument 1: the number to round
#   └───────────── function name

round(25.347, digits = 1)   # 25.3
round(25.347, digits = 0)   # 25
round(25.347)               # 25 (digits defaults to 0)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Arguments can be positional or named.** `round(25.347, 1)` works because R knows the second argument is `digits`. Named arguments (`digits = 1`) are clearer, especially when a function has many arguments.
:::


## Writing Your Own Functions

When you repeat the same calculation, wrap it in a function.

```{r}
#| label: custom-nmr
#| echo: true

# Calculate neonatal mortality rate
calc_nmr <- function(neonatal_deaths, live_births) {
  nmr <- (neonatal_deaths / live_births) * 1000
  return(nmr)
}

# Use it
calc_nmr(neonatal_deaths = 30, live_births = 1200)   # 25.0
calc_nmr(22, 1100)                                    # 20.0
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: custom-mmr
#| echo: true

# Calculate maternal mortality ratio
calc_mmr <- function(maternal_deaths, live_births) {
  mmr <- (maternal_deaths / live_births) * 100000
  return(mmr)
}

# Use it
calc_mmr(maternal_deaths = 3, live_births = 1200)   # 250.0
calc_mmr(5, 950)                                     # 526.3
```


## Functions with Default Arguments

```{r}
#| label: function-defaults
#| echo: true

# Classify NMR with a customizable threshold
classify_nmr <- function(nmr, target = 12) {
  if (nmr <= target) {
    return("On Track")
  } else if (nmr <= target * 2) {
    return("Needs Improvement")
  } else {
    return("Critical")
  }
}

classify_nmr(10)          # "On Track" (uses default target = 12)
classify_nmr(20)          # "Needs Improvement"
classify_nmr(42)          # "Critical"
classify_nmr(20, target = 15)  # "Needs Improvement" (custom target)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Default arguments make functions flexible.** The SDG target for NMR is 12, so that's a sensible default --- but a user can override it for a different analysis.
:::


## Applying Functions to Vectors

Your custom functions work on vectors just like built-in ones.

```{r}
#| label: function-vectorized
#| echo: true

# Data from five facilities
neonatal_deaths <- c(30, 30, 22, 34, 29)
live_births     <- c(1200, 950, 1100, 800, 1050)

# Calculate NMR for all facilities at once
facility_nmr <- calc_nmr(neonatal_deaths, live_births)
facility_nmr
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: function-vectorized-2
#| echo: true

# Name the results
facility_names <- c("Facility A", "Facility B", "Facility C",
                    "Facility D", "Facility E")
names(facility_nmr) <- facility_names

# Identify high-risk facilities
facility_nmr[facility_nmr > 30]
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Functions you write with basic arithmetic are automatically vectorized.** Since `*`, `/`, and other operators work element-wise, `calc_nmr()` works on single values or entire vectors.
:::


## Why Write Functions?

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.bad-example}
[Without functions: repeated code]{.example-label}

```{r}
#| label: no-function
#| echo: true

# Facility A
nmr_a <- (30 / 1200) * 1000

# Facility B
nmr_b <- (30 / 950) * 1000

# Facility C
nmr_c <- (22 / 1100) * 1000

# What if the formula changes?
# Fix it in three places!
```
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.good-example}
[With functions: write once, use everywhere]{.example-label}

```{r}
#| label: with-function
#| echo: true

calc_nmr <- function(deaths, births) {
  (deaths / births) * 1000
}

nmr_a <- calc_nmr(30, 1200)
nmr_b <- calc_nmr(30, 950)
nmr_c <- calc_nmr(22, 1100)

# Formula changes? Fix once.
```
:::
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**The DRY principle: Don't Repeat Yourself.** If you copy-paste the same calculation more than twice, write a function instead.
:::


# Getting Help {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Finding answers when you're stuck</span>


## R's Built-in Help System

```{r}
#| label: help-system
#| echo: true

# Open the help page for a function
?mean
?round
?factor

# Search for a topic (fuzzy search)
??neonatal          # searches all help files for "neonatal"
help.search("standard deviation")

# See examples of a function in action
example(mean)

# See a function's arguments
args(round)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Help pages look intimidating at first.** Focus on three sections: **Description** (what it does), **Arguments** (what inputs it takes), and **Examples** (copy-paste working code).
:::


## Reading a Help Page

The help page for `mean()` contains:

| Section | What It Tells You |
|---------|-------------------|
| **Description** | Computes the arithmetic mean |
| **Usage** | `mean(x, trim = 0, na.rm = FALSE, ...)` |
| **Arguments** | `x` = a numeric vector; `na.rm` = remove NAs? |
| **Value** | Returns a single numeric value |
| **Examples** | Working code you can copy and run |

. . .

```{r}
#| label: help-example
#| echo: true

deliveries <- c(112, 98, NA, 121, NA, 143)

# From the help page, we learn about na.rm
mean(deliveries)                   # NA (not helpful)
mean(deliveries, na.rm = TRUE)     # 118.5 (what we need)
```


## Beyond the Help Page

::: {.incremental}
- **Stack Overflow** --- search for your error message; someone has already asked about it
- **R for Data Science** (r4ds.hadley.nz) --- the definitive free textbook for data analysis in R
- **CRAN Task Views** --- curated lists of packages by topic (epidemiology, spatial, etc.)
- **RStudio Cheatsheets** --- one-page visual references for key packages (File > Help > Cheat Sheets)
- **Posit Community** (community.rstudio.com) --- friendly forum for R questions
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**The best R programmers are not the ones who memorize everything --- they are the ones who know how to search effectively.** When you hit an error, copy the exact error message and search it.
:::


## Debugging Strategies

When something goes wrong --- and it will --- here is a systematic approach.

::: {.incremental}
1. **Read the error message.** R's messages are cryptic but informative. `object 'nmr' not found` means you haven't created `nmr` yet.
2. **Check your types.** Use `class()` and `str()` to verify your data looks the way you expect.
3. **Simplify.** Run your code line by line in the console to find exactly where it breaks.
4. **Search the error message.** Paste the exact error into a search engine.
5. **Ask for help.** Include a minimal reproducible example --- the smallest code that produces the error.
:::


## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaway}
**Vectors are R's fundamental building block.** They store related values and support vectorized operations that replace manual cell-by-cell work.

**Four data types** cover virtually all MNH data: numeric (rates, counts), character (names, IDs), logical (yes/no flags), and factor (ordered categories).

**Custom functions eliminate copy-paste errors.** Write `calc_nmr()` once, use it everywhere, and fix it in one place.

**Getting help is a skill, not a weakness.** Use `?function_name`, read error messages carefully, and search effectively.
:::


## Looking Ahead

::: {.callout-box}
**Next: Chapter 3 --- Importing Data**

- Reading CSV files from DHIS2 exports with `readr`
- Reading Excel files with `readxl`
- Handling common import problems: wrong types, messy headers, encoding issues
- Inspecting your data immediately after import
:::


##  {background-color="white"}

<br><br><br>

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![](bka-logo.png){width="250px"}

<br>

[R for Management Consultants | Chapter 2: R Building Blocks]{style="color: #005CB9; font-size: 0.6em;"}

[BK Advisors]{style="color: #666; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::
